1.synthesis: After the Sabbath, at the dawn of the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary come to see the tomb (v.1). A great earthquake occurs; an angel of the Lord descends from heaven, rolls back the stone, and sits on it — his appearance like lightning, his clothing white as snow (vv.2–3). The guards become like dead men from fear. The angel speaks to the women: do not be afraid; he is risen, as he said; come see the place where he lay; go quickly and tell the disciples he is going before them to Galilee (vv.5–7). They leave in fear and great joy. Jesus meets them as they go: "Greetings" (chairete) — they take hold of his feet and worship him (v.9). He repeats the Galilee directive: tell my brothers to go to Galilee, and there they will see me (v.10). The Resurrection is announced before it is witnessed, witnessed before it is understood, and set in motion before the disciples can process it.
1.historical_context: "The first day of the week" (mia sabbatōn, v.1) — Sunday, the day after the Jewish Sabbath — became the distinctive Christian day of worship precisely because of this morning. The earthquake (v.2) is the second in the Passion account (the first at Jesus's death, 27:51), signaling cosmic disruption at the boundary moments of the Passion. The angel's appearance — "lightning" and "white as snow" — matches Daniel 10:6 and the Transfiguration's description (17:2), confirming that the same divine glory witnessed on the mountain now attends the empty tomb. The guards' prostration ("became like dead men," v.4) mirrors the disciples' prostration at the Transfiguration (17:6), creating a resurrection-as-theophany frame: what the guards experience as terror is what the women and eventually the disciples will experience as worship. The greeting chairete (v.9, "Greetings") is also the word for "Rejoice" — the risen Jesus's first word to his disciples is joy.
1.literary_structure: The resurrection account has a double annunciation: first by the angel (vv.5–7) and then by Jesus himself (vv.9–10), both delivering the same Galilee directive. The women's emotional state — "fear and great joy" (v.8) — captures the paradox of the resurrection encounter: the cognitive content (he is risen, as he said) and the affective impact (the divine presence of the empty tomb) exceed what any ordinary emotional category can hold. The angel's "as he said" (v.6) is the resurrection's hermeneutical key: the empty tomb is not a mystery to be solved but a word to be remembered — Jesus said this would happen, and the empty place where he lay is the evidence that his word holds. The women are commissioned as the first resurrection witnesses before any male disciple — a historicity indicator unlikely to be invented by a first-century author seeking credibility.
1.theological_application: "He is not here, for he has risen, as he said" (v.6). The resurrection's announcement turns on his own prior word: the three passion predictions, the Jonah sign, the Temple-rebuilding claim — all converge on this morning. The Gospel's insistence that the resurrection was predicted before it occurred (and was remembered as such: v.6, "as he said"; v.7, "as he told you") is not a literary convenience but a theological claim: the resurrection is not an accident of divine improvisation but the predetermined completion of the redemptive plan. The command "do not be afraid" (v.5) and Jesus's first word of greeting — "Rejoice" (v.9) — together define the resurrection's pastoral register: death's power over those in Christ has been broken, and the appropriate response to that fact is fearlessness and joy.
1.christology: Jesus's greeting to the women (v.9) and his repetition of the Galilee directive (v.10 — "tell my brothers") are the risen Jesus's first words in the Gospel. He calls the disciples who fled (26:56) and Peter who denied (26:74) "my brothers" — the post-resurrection community is defined by fraternal belonging, not pre-Passion faithfulness. The risen Jesus is recognizable (they take hold of his feet; they worship him) and commanding (he gives the same instruction the angel gave) — a continuous identity between the crucified and the risen. The Galilee directive restores the geography of the ministry: the disciples are to return to the place of the calling and the teaching, where the risen Lord will meet them and commission them for the world. The mountain in Galilee where they will see him is the site of the Gospel's final and fullest christological claim.

11.synthesis: While the women are on their way, some of the guard go into the city and report everything that happened to the chief priests (v.11). The priests assemble with the elders, take counsel, and give the soldiers a sufficient sum of money: tell people the disciples came by night and stole the body while we were sleeping (vv.12–13). If Pilate hears of it, they will satisfy him and keep them out of trouble (v.14). The soldiers take the money and do as directed; this story spreads among the Jews and is still told "to this day" (v.15). The narrative of the stolen body — which requires the guards to confess to sleeping at their post, a capital offense under Roman military law — is the institutional response to an empty tomb: not denial that the tomb is empty but a counter-narrative about why it is empty.
11.historical_context: Matthew's note that "this story has been spread among the Jews to this day" (v.15) is a contemporary reference, indicating the narrative addresses a live polemic between Matthew's community and the synagogue, probably in the 80s CE. Justin Martyr (Dialogue with Trypho 108, ca. 150 CE) records that the Jewish community was still circulating this counter-narrative — confirming that the empty tomb was not disputed but interpreted differently. The guards' admission of sleep (v.13) is self-incriminating: Roman soldiers who slept on duty faced decimation or execution; the bribe and Pilate's protection are thus their only protection against their own testimony. The institutional decision to fund a lie rather than investigate the empty tomb's cause is the leaders' final act in Matthew's Gospel — exactly mirroring the Sanhedrin's opening decision to arrest Jesus by stealth rather than confront his teaching publicly (26:4).
11.literary_structure: The guard report (vv.11–15) is the narrative complement to the guard at the tomb (27:62–66): the institutional precaution that was meant to prevent the resurrection's proclamation becomes, in its failure, its most unintentional confirmation. The leaders know the tomb is empty (they receive the report firsthand, v.11); they have no body; they can only manage the narrative. "They gave a sufficient sum of money" (v.12) echoes the thirty pieces given to Judas (26:15) — institutional money deployed to suppress the truth replaces institutional money deployed to eliminate the truth-teller. Both payments fail. The section ends with the guard narrative spreading while the disciples are summoned to Galilee — two responses to the same empty tomb moving in opposite directions.
11.theological_application: The counter-narrative ("his disciples came by night and stole him away," v.13) is the original alternative to the resurrection claim and remains its structural form in every generation: the evidence is not denied but explained away. Matthew preserves this counter-narrative not to ridicule it but to note that the empty tomb's reality was not disputed even by those most motivated to dispute it. The leaders' institutional response to the resurrection — manage the narrative, pay the witnesses, suppress the report — models the alternative to faith: the choice to protect existing power structures against the disruptive claim that the crucified king is alive. This is the institutional form of what the Olivet Discourse called the "cares of the world" that choke the word: not denial but management.
11.christology: The stolen-body narrative inadvertently confirms the central resurrection claim: the body is gone. The leaders' chosen explanation — theft by frightened, scattered disciples — is less credible than the resurrection the guards reported, but it is the only available alternative. The risen Jesus is at this point meeting the women (vv.9–10) and directing the disciples to Galilee while his institutional opponents are devising the counter-narrative. The two movements — the risen Christ sending his people to the nations, and the institutional leadership paying soldiers to deny it — are the two responses to the Easter morning that have continued through all subsequent history. Matthew records both without comment, because the empty tomb speaks for itself.

16.synthesis: The eleven disciples go to Galilee, to the mountain Jesus appointed. When they see him they worship — but some doubt (vv.16–17). Jesus approaches and speaks: "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you. And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age" (vv.18–20). The Gospel ends where it began — with a named presence of God (Matthew 1:23: "God with us") — and where the Sermon on the Mount ended (7:28, the crowds were astonished at his authority). All authority, all nations, all commands, always: the Great Commission's four "alls" are the risen Lord's charter for the church's existence until the age's close.
16.historical_context: The Galilean mountain (the specific mountain is unnamed — all suggested identifications are traditional) gathers the threads of the Gospel's mountain theology: the Sermon on the Mount (ch5), the Transfiguration (17:1), and now the Commission. Mountains in Matthew are consistently the sites of authoritative revelation. "All authority in heaven and on earth" (exousia, v.18) is a claim grounded in Daniel 7:14: "to him was given dominion and glory and a kingdom, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him." The Trinitarian formula — "in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" (v.19) — is the earliest complete Trinitarian formulation in the NT; baptism into the tri-personal Name is entrance into the covenant community. The promise of presence (v.20) echoes the Immanuel name of 1:23 and Jesus's promise in 18:20 ("where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them").
16.literary_structure: The Commission is structured as the Gospel's final chiasm: authority (foundation) → mission command (go, make disciples, baptize, teach) → presence (guarantee). The mission command uses a single imperative ("make disciples," mathēteusate) with three participles as its means (going, baptizing, teaching) — discipleship-making is the commission's heart; the other actions are its instruments. "All nations" (v.19) reverses the restriction of 10:5–6 ("go nowhere among the Gentiles") — the Israel-first priority of the earthly ministry is superseded by the universal scope of the resurrection commission. The teaching command (v.20, "teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you") creates a direct link from the Great Commission back to the five discourses: the teaching that Matthew has recorded is the substance of what the church is to teach all nations. The Gospel ends not with a scene but a promise — the last word is presence.
16.theological_application: "And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age" (v.20). The risen Jesus does not send the disciples into the nations and depart; he sends them and goes with them. This is the Gospel's final answer to the disciples' recurring anxiety — the little faith of the storms, the bread-worries of the lake crossing, the sleep of Gethsemane, the flight at the arrest. The presence of the risen Lord is the resource that makes the impossible mission possible: not their competence, their courage, or their numbers, but his authority (v.18) and his presence (v.20). The promise "to the end of the age" connects the Commission directly to the Olivet Discourse's eschatological horizon: the disciples are sent out into the period between the Resurrection and the Parousia, and the presence that was with them in Galilee and Jerusalem will be with them in every nation until the Son of Man comes on the clouds.
16.christology: "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" (v.18). The claim is total and unprecedented: not some authority, not earthly authority only, but cosmic authority — the authority of the Creator over all things in every realm. This is the Daniel 7:14 Son of Man's authority made explicit in the voice of the risen Jesus on a Galilean mountain. The one who was handed over to Pilate now claims authority over Pilate, Caesar, and every power in heaven and earth. The authority grounds the mission: the church goes to all nations not on its own initiative or with its own power but as the extension of the risen Lord's universal reign. And the promise — "I am with you always" — identifies him as the Immanuel of 1:23 not only in the Incarnation but in the church's ongoing life. The last verse of Matthew's Gospel is the first verse of the church's story.
